---
title: "Theory"
subtitle: "The intuition behind the construction — condensed from the full paper"
---

This page is a web-friendly walkthrough of the ideas behind `grocery-sim`,
condensed but not watered down — every equation below is copied from the
same model that generates the data, not a simplified stand-in for it. For
the complete derivations, every calibrated parameter, and the causal-graph
figures, see
[the full paper (PDF)](https://github.com/ChinhMaiGit/grocery-sim/blob/main/paper/paper.pdf).

## The problem with the usual two options

Hand an analyst a real business's spreadsheet, and she can check whether her
conclusions sound reasonable — but not whether they are *true*, because
nobody, not her, not the business itself, actually knows the mechanism that
produced the numbers. Hand her a spreadsheet generated by drawing every
column from a convenient distribution, and she can check her work against
the generating code, but there's nothing underneath worth discovering: no
owner who misread a signal, no competitor who mattered less than everyone
assumed, no honest mess in the paperwork to clean up before the real
question can even be asked.

`grocery-sim` is a third option: build the business first, as a world with
people, budgets, and a street, and only afterward let the paperwork fall out
of what that world actually did. The result is a dataset messy enough to
feel real, where every number still has a traceable cause — a conclusion can
be checked not just for plausibility but for truth.

## Two layers, and the rule that never gets broken

The world is built from two layers that are never allowed to blend into one
another:

- **The micro layer — decisions.** A customer choosing between two cartons
  of milk. An owner choosing where to open a store before a single customer
  has walked through the door. The same owner choosing, later, whether to
  hire, and whether to expand, under capital he does not have room to lose.
  Every one of these is a real decision, built from plain human intuition
  first and only then written as a precise mathematical object.
- **The macro layer — the script.** The weather, the calendar, and the
  macroeconomic shocks are scripted in advance and never listen to anything
  the simulated people do. A supply shock happens on a fixed date whether or
  not the owner reacts well to it.

The distinction is not a modeling convenience — it's the whole point. It's
what lets a later reader ask *what would have happened without the storm, or
without the competitor*, and get an answer that is **exactly true rather
than merely estimated.**

## The customer's purchase decision

Every product $s$ offers customer $i$ a utility

$$
U_{is} = \theta_{ic} + a_s + \gamma\left(1 - \left|b_i -
\text{BrandLevel}_s\right|\right) - \beta_i\,\text{Price}_s ,
$$

where $\theta_{ic}$ is her current need for the category, $b_i$ is her
brand affinity, $\text{BrandLevel}_s$ is the product's position on that
same spectrum, $\gamma$ sets how much a good brand match is worth, $\beta_i$
is her own price sensitivity, and $a_s \sim \text{Normal}(0, 0.8)$ is an
unmodeled per-product appeal — packaging, familiarity, the thousand reasons
one product outsells its shelf neighbor for no principled reason at all.
Alongside every stocked product sits an outside option (buy nothing here,
or buy elsewhere), and the actual choice is drawn as

$$
\text{choice} \sim \text{Categorical}\big(\text{softmax}(U_{is_1}, \ldots,
U_{is_K}, U_0)\big) .
$$

The softmax form isn't a convenient approximation. If each product's
utility is disturbed by an independent Gumbel shock and the customer simply
picks the option with the highest noisy utility, the resulting choice
probabilities are *exactly* the softmax of the mean utilities — McFadden's
random utility result. It's also exactly the model a real discrete-choice
analyst would fit to purchase histories, so grading a later exercise
against the true parameters is a fair comparison of like against like.

Need itself, $\theta_{ict}$, is not a free-floating random draw — it comes
from a pantry that drains and refills:

$$
P_{ic,t+1} \overset{\leftarrow}{=} \max(0,\ P_{ict} - r_{ict}) +
\text{units purchased}, \qquad
\theta_{ict} = \alpha\,\frac{\max(0,\ P^{\text{tgt}}_{ic} -
P_{ict})}{P^{\text{tgt}}_{ic}} ,
$$

the shortfall against a target of ten days' comfortable cover. The drain
rate $r_{ict}$ scales with the season's demand modifier rather than adding
to desire directly, so a hot week doesn't make a household want ice cream
in the abstract — it makes the household eat the ice cream in the freezer
faster. When the chosen product is out of stock, the original intent is
logged as a latent truth under cause `stockout` before the softmax is
re-evaluated with that product removed — an honest ledger of demand that
never appears in the sales data at all.

## The owner's opening decision

The true demand potential of a location for a category is
$D_{cl} = \text{Households}_l \cdot \mu_c$ — ground truth nobody in the
model observes directly. The owner's belief is that truth seen through a
noisy, mildly optimistic lens,

$$
\hat{D}_{cl} = D_{cl}\,e^{\delta_c}, \qquad \delta_c \sim
\text{Normal}(0.10,\ 0.25) ,
$$

on the log scale because people misjudge quantities in ratios, not
absolute units, and the positive mean encodes a documented behavioral
regularity: new owners tend to be mildly overoptimistic. That single
choice is what causes the store to systematically overstock in its early
months — the size of the overstocking is computable exactly from the
belief error, not asserted as an unexplained fact about the data.

With beliefs, costs, and a handful of standing policies fixed — a markup
$m_c$, a safety-stock fraction $\eta = 0.30$ of believed monthly demand, a
cap $\rho = 0.05$ on any one product's share of its category — the opening
decision is a mixed integer linear program:

$$
\max \;\; \sum_{c}\sum_{s \in S_c} (1+m_c)\,\text{Cost}_s \cdot u_s \;-\;
\text{TotalCost}_0
$$

subject to, among others,

$$
\sum_l y_l = 1, \qquad
u_s \leq q_s, \qquad
\sum_{s \in S_c} u_s \leq \sum_l y_l\,\hat{D}_{cl}, \qquad
\text{TotalCost}_0 \leq \text{InitialCapital}_0 ,
$$

with $y_l \in \{0,1\}$ the chosen location, $q_s \geq 0$ the quantity
purchased, and $u_s \geq 0$ the quantity the owner believes he'll sell. A
mixed integer program is the right tool because the decision is genuinely
joint: location and listing choices are binary, purchase quantities are
continuous, and every constraint linking them is linear once the location
is fixed. Because the belief error carries a positive mean, the optimum
systematically overstocks relative to the truth — so when an analyst later
finds bloated inventory in the cost sheets, there's a real mechanism behind
it, not an injected anomaly.

## Day-to-day adaptive policy

Once the shop is open, restocking is sized off a trailing four-week average
of the owner's own recorded sales,

$$
\hat{D}^{\text{op}}_{cw} \overset{\leftarrow}{=} \frac{1}{4}\sum_{j=1}^{4}
\text{Sales}_{c,w-j} ,
\qquad
\text{Target}_{sw} = \left(\tfrac{R+L}{7} + \eta\,\tfrac{30}{7}\right)
\hat{D}^{\text{op}}_{cw}\,\tilde{\sigma}_{sw} ,
$$

with $R = 7$ days between reviews and $L = 2$ days of lead time. The rule
carries two realistic flaws by construction: it's backward-looking, so a
rising trend is always underestimated by the average of the weeks before
it rose, and it's blind to censoring, since the sales it averages are
themselves truncated by the store's own past stockouts — a product that
stocks out repeatedly reports weak sales, earns a smaller share of the
next order, and stocks out again.

Perishable stock spoils overnight,

$$
\text{Spoiled}_{st} \sim \text{Binomial}\big(\text{OnHand}_{st},\
\min(0.35,\ \lambda^d_{c(s)}\,f_{c(s),t})\big) ,
$$

with the spoilage factor $f_{ct}$ built log-additively from a heat term
(strongest for ambient perishables), a cold-chain strain term tied to the
macro shocks below, and an idiosyncratic batch-luck term.

## The expansion decision

From the start of year two, the owner retains a share $\rho_{RE} = 0.5$ of
every profitable month's after-tax result, drawing the rest as income and
neither drawing nor retaining anything in a loss month:

$$
\text{draw}_m = (1-\rho_{RE})\,\max(0, \pi_m) .
$$

Once retained earnings *and* available cash both cross fixed thresholds,

$$
\text{RE}_m \geq K_{\text{exp}}, \qquad
\text{cash}_m - \text{tax\_reserve}_m \geq C_{\text{exp}} ,
$$

the owner expands on the first day of the following month. This is a
threshold decision framed around what he can afford to lose, not a
discounted-cash-flow forecast — closer to what entrepreneurship research
calls *effectuation* than to a textbook net-present-value calculation. A
counterfactual twin that disables the threshold entirely shows the
expansion's true cost across its first fourteen months substantially
exceeds the margin the extra capacity generates: the fixed cost of an
extra wage is paid in full while the extra revenue is diluted by the
ordinary grocery margin.

## The macro layer: weather, demand, and cost shocks

Temperature follows a deterministic annual cycle plus a persistent
stochastic anomaly,

$$
T_t = \mu_T(t) + \varepsilon_t, \qquad
\mu_T(t) = 12 + 9\cos\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi(t-200)}{365}\right), \qquad
\varepsilon_t = \phi\,\varepsilon_{t-1} + \text{Normal}(0,\sigma_\varepsilon) ,
$$

with $\phi = 0.7$ — an autoregressive form chosen because it's the maximum
entropy stationary process consistent with the two facts about weather
worth encoding: a stable typical spread, and warm or cold spells that
persist for days rather than resetting each morning.

A daily, per-category demand modifier is built log-additively from three
genuinely distinct causal channels,

$$
\log M_{ct} = a_c \cos\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi(t-200)}{365}\right) +
\kappa_c\, z_t + h_c\, H_t ,
$$

where $z_t$ is the standardized temperature anomaly and $H_t$ flags days
before a major holiday: $a_c$ is the predictable seasonal amplitude,
$\kappa_c$ the pure weather effect, $h_c$ the holiday stock-up surge. One
category is deliberately given all-zero loadings, so nothing in
preferences makes it more or less wanted at any time of year — yet its
observed sales still aren't flat, because every category draws from the
same household budget, and other categories' seasonal surges crowd it out.
An analyst who stops at an apparent seasonal pattern here has mistaken a
budget-mediation effect for a genuine preference effect.

Wholesale cost shocks arrive as a marked point process, $K \sim
\text{Poisson}(1.5)$ events at $\tau_k \sim \text{Uniform}(1,365)$, each
with peak magnitude $\zeta_k \sim \text{LogNormal}(\log\zeta_0,\ 0.3)$ and
a ramp-up/decay trajectory

$$
g_k(t) = \zeta_k \cdot \min\!\left(1,\ \tfrac{t-\tau_k}{14}\right) \cdot
\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(t-\tau_k-14)_+}{\rho_k}\right) ,
$$

ramping over two weeks and decaying exponentially after — neither a
permanent level change nor a transient blip, but a slow wave a forecaster
genuinely has to track. Every affected category's cost multiplier then
assembles the inflation drift and every live shock,

$$
\log \text{CostMult}_{ct} = \frac{\pi t}{365} +
\sum_k g_k(t)\,\mathbb{1}[c \in A_k] .
$$

The owner never observes this directly — he sees only supplier invoices,
tracked as an exponentially smoothed trend, and moves the shelf tag only
once that trend has drifted more than three percent from the current price
(menu-cost pricing). The scripted energy crisis also squeezes household
budgets through a separate mechanism, making it a subtly *invalid*
instrument whose bias is, in principle, computable exactly — a trap left
in on purpose.

## Taxation

Value-added tax applies at a category-specific rate $r_c(t)$; a flat
$20\%$ profit tax applies only to a positive annual surplus,

$$
\text{ProfitTax} = r^{\pi}\cdot\max(0,\ \text{ProfitBeforeTax}), \qquad
r^\pi = 0.20 .
$$

One piece of this is provably harmless rather than merely assumed away:
because profit tax is a flat rate on a positive base,

$$
\arg\max_{\text{decisions}} (1-r^\pi)\,\pi(\text{decisions}) =
\arg\max_{\text{decisions}} \pi(\text{decisions}) ,
$$

so an owner who ignores profit tax when making ordinary operating
decisions loses nothing relative to one who optimizes around it. No such
guarantee holds for value-added tax, whose category-specific rate changes
relative prices across categories — a gap the owner never exploits,
deliberately left open for a later prescriptive analysis to find.

## Why the split makes counterfactuals free

Because the macro layer never listens to the micro layer, a counterfactual
world costs almost nothing to produce: edit the script — delete the
scripted shock, disable the expansion threshold, remove the competitor
entirely — and replay the identical daily market mechanism with every
other random draw held fixed. The difference between the original run and
the edited run *is* the causal effect of the edit, not merely an estimate
of it.

This only works because every random draw is **keyed by the stable
identity of the entity it concerns** — a specific customer on a specific
day, a specific product on a specific night — using independently spawned
child streams, rather than one long sequential stream. If draws were
sequential, deleting a single event partway through the year would shift
every subsequent draw, contaminating the comparison with reshuffled
randomness that has nothing to do with the edit. Keying every draw by
identity means a customer untouched by the edit reproduces the identical
decision, bit for bit, in both runs — the **common random numbers** (CRN)
technique from the simulation literature, load-bearing rather than
retrofitted.

Two concrete twins in the three-year story cash this out into real
numbers: one twin disables the expansion decision entirely; comparing it
to the real run shows the expansion's true cost over its first fourteen
months substantially exceeds the margin it generates. A second twin
removes the competitor entirely; comparing it to the real run prices
exactly what the competitor cost the owner, as opposed to what he
*believes* it cost him. The capital decision, it turns out, very often
costs the business more than the competitive entry — even though the
competitor is the more visible, more emotionally salient event to an owner
living through it. That's exactly why an instructor can hand a learner the
owner's own conviction that the competitor is to blame, and let the twins
settle the question instead.

## The three profit figures

With a full year performed, three profit figures become computable side by
side:

- $\pi_{\text{believed}}$ — the opening MILP's own objective value.
- $\pi_{\text{realized}}$ — what the ledger actually shows, after a full
  year of realistic behavior.
- $\pi_{\text{oracle}}$ — what a perfectly informed operator could have
  earned: same world, same script, true demand in place of a censored,
  backward-looking forecast.

The two gaps between them are exact differences between fully computed runs
of the same mechanism, not estimates:

$$
\Delta_{\text{optimism}} = \pi_{\text{realized}} - \pi_{\text{believed}}
\qquad
\Delta_{\text{analytics}} = \pi_{\text{oracle}} - \pi_{\text{realized}}
$$

The first measures the cost of the owner's initial overoptimism; the second
measures the value of better information and better decisions — the single
number this entire construction exists to make credible by actually
computing it, rather than asserting a plausible size for it.

## The recording layer's own algebra

The paperwork sits in a third layer, neither decision nor script: low-rate,
independently keyed defects injected only at export, never touching the
simulation's internal state. Let $D_{st}$ be a product's cumulative
book-minus-true discrepancy on day $t$ — pushed up by duplicate invoices
and unrecorded spoilage, down by missing invoices and duplicate receipts.
The inventory figure an analyst actually sees is book stock, not true
stock,

$$
B_{st} = S_{st} + D_{st} - \sum_{c\le t} A_{sc} ,
$$

where $A_{sc}$ is the cumulative correction posted at each monthly
physical count, snapping book stock back to physical truth before it
begins drifting anew. Every defect is written simultaneously to a
separate answer key, so reconciling the messy records back to the truth is
itself gradable against a known answer — the underlying simulated reality
this section's own machinery depends on is never disturbed by paperwork
noise that exists only in the documents an analyst receives.

## Read more

- The [Analysis catalog](analysis-catalog.qmd) turns this theory into a
  concrete, gradeable set of questions.
- The full paper covers the customer's purchase decision, the owner's
  opening MILP, adaptive day-to-day policy, the expansion decision, the
  customer panel as a stochastic flow, and the entire macro script (calendar,
  weather, cost shocks, taxation) in complete derivations —
  [read it here](https://github.com/ChinhMaiGit/grocery-sim/blob/main/paper/paper.pdf).
